Paper: Creative Robot Tool Use by Counterfactual Reasoning
Core reason: Closest paper using controlled physical counterfactuals for tool reasoning.
What it supports: Counterfactual perturbation of tool properties can expose causal suitability features.
What it does NOT support: It does not learn direct-manipulation versus tool-use necessity.

Paper: Robustness-Aware Tool Selection and Manipulation Planning with Learned Energy-Informed Guidance
Core reason: Strong evidence that tool choice and tool state affect contact robustness.
What it supports: Tool suitability and robustness should influence selection and planning.
What it does NOT support: It does not include no-tool as a decision option or learn necessity.

Paper: Physics-Conditioned Grasping for Stable Tool Use
Core reason: Shows that task-conditioned tool physics changes the correct grasp.
What it supports: Tool state and interaction wrench can alter downstream action choices.
What it does NOT support: It does not address whether a tool should be used or mobile-base decisions.

Paper: CoPa: General Robotic Manipulation through Spatial Constraints of Parts with Foundation Models
Core reason: Useful modular spatial-grounding comparison but not specific to necessity.
What it supports: Part grounding and spatial constraints can convert language into executable poses.
What it does NOT support: It does not provide tool/no-tool labels or tool-state interventions.

Paper: Creative Robot Tool Use with Large Language Models
Core reason: Representative language-planning and skill-library tool-use route.
What it supports: A non-end-to-end planner can reason over tasks designed to require tools.
What it does NOT support: It does not establish learned necessity from paired visual states.

Paper: RT-Affordance: Affordances are Versatile Intermediate Representations for Robot Manipulation
Core reason: Defines a strong boundary between affordance guidance and necessity decisions.
What it supports: Affordance plans are useful intermediate supervision for how to manipulate.
What it does NOT support: Affordance prediction alone does not prove a tool is necessary or available.

Paper: AffordGrasp: In-Context Affordance Reasoning for Open-Vocabulary Task-Oriented Grasping in Clutter
Core reason: Practical affordance-only and task-oriented grasp comparison.
What it supports: VLM reasoning can ground functional regions and select task-oriented grasps.
What it does NOT support: It does not evaluate direct versus tool-use decisions.

Paper: Learning Precise Affordances from Egocentric Videos for Robotic Manipulation
Core reason: Shows how video can provide affordance supervision without action labels.
What it supports: Human video can support functional-region and grasp supervision.
What it does NOT support: It does not provide robot-specific necessity or mobile feasibility labels.

Paper: OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model
Core reason: Reproducible no-explicit-tool-awareness VLA reference.
What it supports: A general VLA can serve as the common image-language backbone.
What it does NOT support: It does not show causal use of tool state or necessity supervision.

Paper: Octo: An Open-Source Generalist Robot Policy
Core reason: Lighter open generalist policy suitable as an alternative backbone.
What it supports: Cross-embodiment policy pretraining and efficient adaptation.
What it does NOT support: It does not provide tool necessity labels or intervention evaluation.

Paper: π0.5: a Vision-Language-Action Model with Open-World Generalization
Core reason: Useful capability reference but not a practical first-stage baseline.
What it supports: Large heterogeneous co-training can improve open-world manipulation.
What it does NOT support: It does not isolate tool state or tool necessity.

Paper: GR00T N1: An Open Foundation Model for Generalist Humanoid Robots
Core reason: Background evidence that synthetic and human data can be mixed in a foundation policy.
What it supports: Heterogeneous real human and synthetic data can support general manipulation.
What it does NOT support: It does not validate AIGC for tool necessity or quadruped tool decisions.

Paper: Unified World Models: Coupling Video and Action Diffusion for Pretraining on Large Robotic Datasets
Core reason: Representative joint future-video and action-modeling comparison.
What it supports: Action-free video can be integrated with action learning through a unified model.
What it does NOT support: It does not show that future prediction learns intent or necessity.

Paper: Video Generators are Robot Policies
Core reason: Representative generated-video plus action policy route.
What it supports: Video generation can be coupled to action decoding and policy learning.
What it does NOT support: Generated video is not automatically valid action supervision or tool-state evidence.

Paper: DreamGen: Unlocking Generalization in Robot Learning through Video World Models
Core reason: Direct reference for synthetic robot videos and recovered pseudo-actions.
What it supports: Video world models can generate neural trajectories for policy training.
What it does NOT support: It does not guarantee contact physics or produce necessity labels by default.

Paper: Gen2Act: Human Video Generation in Novel Scenarios enables Generalizable Robot Manipulation
Core reason: Useful example of generated video as high-level behavior guidance.
What it supports: Generated human video can provide motion priors for novel tasks.
What it does NOT support: It does not guarantee robot reachability or direct action supervision.

Paper: EMMA: Generalizing Real-World Robot Manipulation via Generative Visual Transfer
Core reason: Primarily visual-domain augmentation rather than necessity supervision.
What it supports: AIGC can expand visual appearance while attempting to preserve geometry.
What it does NOT support: It does not create new physical counterfactual labels.

Paper: AHA: A Vision-Language-Model for Detecting and Reasoning Over Failures in Robotic Manipulation
Core reason: Strong precedent for generating negative examples by perturbing successful trajectories.
What it supports: Procedural failure synthesis can create scalable failure supervision.
What it does NOT support: It addresses execution failure rather than pre-action tool necessity.

Paper: Learning Actionable Manipulation Recovery via Counterfactual Failure Synthesis
Core reason: Closest generative paired-failure and filtering methodology.
What it supports: Generated counterfactual rollouts need semantic visual and kinematic verification.
What it does NOT support: It learns recovery after failure rather than initial tool-use mode selection.

Paper: MoManipVLA: Transferring Vision-language-action Models for General Mobile Manipulation
Core reason: Defines the high-level waypoint to base-arm feasibility interface.
What it supports: Fixed-base VLA outputs require mobile base-arm coordination.
What it does NOT support: It does not support tool-awareness novelty or AIGC effectiveness.

Paper: UMI on Legs: Making Manipulation Policies Mobile with Manipulation-Centric Whole-body Controllers
Core reason: Clean reference for separating manipulation policy from quadruped WBC.
What it supports: End-effector trajectories can interface high-level policy with whole-body control.
What it does NOT support: It does not test tool necessity or tool-state decisions.

Paper: RoboDuet: Learning a Cooperative Policy for Whole-body Legged Loco-Manipulation
Core reason: Supports whole-body coordination as an execution challenge.
What it supports: Leg and arm policies require coordinated tracking on quadruped manipulators.
What it does NOT support: It does not support necessity novelty or generated-data claims.
